topic.what: Historical review tracing the discovery of Helicobacter pylori and the resulting paradigm shift in gastric disease — from the entrenched stress/acid hypersecretion model to recognition of H. pylori as the dominant infectious etiology of peptic ulcer disease and gastritis.
topic.how: Narrative historical analysis drawing on primary discovery papers, Nobel Prize documentation, contemporaneous clinical literature, and reflective accounts from key investigators including Marshall and Warren; contextualization within the sociology of scientific paradigm change.
topic.why_matters: Understanding how a dominant biomedical paradigm was overturned by a single organism discovery informs the epistemology of gastric disease research and illustrates the resistance-to-adoption curve relevant to any disruptive medical finding — including current microbiome and TCM mechanistic work.

characteristics.what_it_measures: Breadth of clinical practice changes necessitated by H. pylori discovery
characteristics.value: Eradication therapy replaced acid suppression as primary curative treatment for peptic ulcer; gastric cancer risk reclassified as infectious/preventable
characteristics.vs_baseline: Pre-1983 acid hypersecretion model and stress ulcer paradigm
characteristics.evidence: Historical audit of treatment guideline changes and mortality data post-eradication era
characteristics.context: Paradigm shift complete within ~15 years of original isolation (1983 Marshall/Warren)

characteristics.what_it_measures: Time between initial discovery and mainstream clinical acceptance
characteristics.value: Approximately 10-15 years from isolation (1983) to widespread eradication guidelines (~1994 NIH Consensus Conference)
characteristics.vs_baseline: Typical major paradigm shift timelines in medicine
characteristics.evidence: Timeline of landmark publications, conference statements, and Nobel Prize (2005)
characteristics.context: Resistance partly due to commercial interests in acid-suppression drugs and difficulty culturing organism

good_for: Researchers and clinicians seeking historical grounding for current H. pylori management guidelines
Science historians and sociologists studying medical paradigm change
Medical educators teaching evidence-based medicine and the hazards of entrenched dogma
Researchers in TCM and microbiome fields navigating similar adoption resistance
not_for: Practical clinical decision-making about H. pylori eradication regimens (outdated for current resistance patterns)
Molecular mechanistic research on H. pylori pathogenesis
requires: Access to historical primary literature for full contextualization

novelty: Comprehensive historiographic account linking the H. pylori discovery arc to Kuhnian paradigm shift theory
Contextualizes the social and commercial resistance to the infectious model — relevant meta-lesson for current microbiome and integrative medicine research
open_problems: Subset of peptic ulcers and gastritis cases not attributable to H. pylori remain mechanistically unexplained
Whether H. pylori eradication eliminates gastric cancer risk in patients with pre-existing atrophy is still debated
Global antibiotic resistance threatening current first-line eradication regimens — new paradigm may again be needed
